title: Pyrography sycamore name plaque
subtitle: A personalised name plaque burned and shaded onto a sycamore board
excerpt: A name plaque in pyrography on sycamore is a complete beginner project: a single name or short phrase, burned with a writing tip and given a simple decorative border or sprig motif, finished with Danish oil. Sycamore is the best species for this project because its pale, fine-grained surface provides maximum contrast with the burned letters and does not have the unpredictable grain patterns that complicate lettering on oak or ash. This beginner project covers layout, lettering, adding a simple border, and finishing.
sourceNotes: Handicraft for Girls (1914) by Idabelle McGlauflin for pyrography lettering and name plaque designs. The Art Amateur vol. 47 (1902) for sycamore as the preferred pyrography ground.

glossaryTerms.definition: A fine-pointed pyrography nib for lettering and line work. Produces a consistent line width at a consistent movement speed.
A round-ended pyrography tip used for dotted textures, stippling, and small decorative motifs. Does not produce a drawn line; instead burns small circular marks.
Fully dried timber. Pyrography requires fully seasoned wood; any moisture in the blank will cause steam to form under the burning tip and produce pale, inconsistent marks.

content.text: Sycamore is the pyrographer's preferred ground for lettering: pale, even-grained, and fine enough in texture to show a clean burned line without the surface grain competing with the letter outlines. A name plaque on
seasoned
sycamore requires only a solid-tip burner with a
writing tip
; the optional
ball stylus
adds a stippled texture to border areas and small decorative motifs. The result is a useful household item and a complete introduction to pyrography as a technique.
What you need: wood

A sycamore board, 20 to 25 cm long, 8 to 10 cm tall, 10 to 12 mm thick, sanded to 400 grit. Edges can be left square, chamfered, or rounded with sandpaper before burning. A pre-sanded blank from a pyrography supplier is the quickest starting point; alternatively, use a length of planed sycamore PAR (planed all round) from a timber merchant.
What you need: tools
A solid-tip pyrography burner with a writing tip. A cooling stand. A pencil for layout. Danish oil for the finish. A hand drill and 4 mm bit for the hanging hole (optional).
Safety
Work with a window open or a fan behind the work; burning produces smoke. Rest the tip on the cooling stand between passes. Do not leave a heated burner unattended.
The work
Step 1: Lay out the text

Draw two parallel pencil lines across the board: the baseline and the cap height. For a name plaque, a cap height of 15 to 20 mm is readable at arm's length. Print the name in capital letters between the lines, centring the text by measuring the total letter length and positioning accordingly. Keep letters 3 to 4 mm apart; closer spacing makes the text harder to read when burned. Leave at least 15 mm margin above, below, and on each side of the text.
Step 2: Burn the letters

Heat the burner and test on scrap sycamore. At the right heat, a medium-speed stroke produces a warm mid-brown line. Burn each letter in separate strokes: vertical strokes first, then horizontals, then diagonals. Keep the tip moving at the same speed throughout each stroke; pause-and-start produces dark blobs at the start of each stroke. The pencil guide lines will burn away as you work over them; the burned lines replace them.
Step 3: Add a border or motif

A simple rectangular border burned 5 mm in from the board edge frames the name and completes the plaque. Burn each border line in a single long stroke. A small decorative motif (a sprig of leaves, a simple flower) can be added in one corner using the writing tip for outlines and the ball stylus for stippled fill in the leaf shapes. Mark the motif lightly in pencil before burning.
Step 4: Finish and hang

Erase any remaining pencil marks gently. Apply one coat of Danish oil to front, back, and edges; wipe off after 15 minutes. A second coat after 24 hours. Bore a 4 mm hanging hole 10 mm down from the top edge, centred, if the plaque is to be wall-mounted. Thread a length of cord through the hole.
Variations

A date or short inscription below the name uses a smaller letter height (8 to 10 mm) and the same technique. Italic letters at this scale are harder to burn evenly; stick to printed capitals for a first attempt.
A lime plaque carves more easily than sycamore if the edges are to be shaped; lime can be chamfered or rounded with a sloyd knife before burning.
Care
Wipe with a dry cloth. The Danish oil finish is adequate for indoor use. Re-apply a thin coat of Danish oil if the surface is scratched.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Problems on a pyrography name plaque.

items.symptom: The letters are not centred on the board
items.cause: The layout was not measured before burning.
items.fix: Measure the total text width in pencil on a separate piece of paper, then transfer the measurement to find the centre point on the board. Always measure and mark before burning.

items.symptom: The burned lines are pale and inconsistent
items.cause: The wood was not fully dry, or the heat setting was too low.
items.fix: Check that the sycamore blank is fully dry (kiln-dried stock from a supplier is reliable; reclaimed or green wood is not suitable). Increase the heat setting one step and test again on scrap.
